---
title: "From *'it worked on my notebook'* to production-ready machine learning"
subtitle: "Bridging the gap with MLOps"
institute: Insee
author: "Lino Galiana"
date: "2025-09-29"
description: |
  GT2 — September 2025
slide-tone: false
chalkboard: false
jump-to-slide: true
mouse-wheel: false
transition: slide
fig-cap-location: bottom
embed-resources: false
custom-toc: true
execute:
  cache: true
---

# Introduction

## The "production wall"

- The majority of data-driven projects [**never deliver value**]{.blue2} ([1](https://sloanreview.mit.edu/projects/expanding-ais-impact-with-organizational-learning/), [2](https://hdsr.mitpress.mit.edu/pub/2fu65ujf/release/6), [3](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/346647451_Beyond_the_Hype_Why_Do_Data-Driven_Projects_Fail))
  + Most *data science* and IA POCs fail when going to production
  + How to [**move beyond the experimentation stage**]{.orange}?

. . .

- [**Going to production**]{.orange}: making an application [**live**]{.blue2} in the space of its [**users**]{.blue2}
    - [**Serving**]{.orange}: [**deploying**]{.blue2} the application in a [**relevant format**]{.blue2} for its potential users
    - [**Keeping it alive**]{.orange}: managing the [**lifecycle**]{.blue2} and fostering [**continuous improvement**]{.blue2}

. . .

- [**Multiple dimensions**]{.orange}: domain knowledge, organisation, infrastructure, technical tooling...


## Fostering continuity

![Source: [ibm.com](https://public.dhe.ibm.com/software/data/sw-library/analytics/data-science-lifecycle/#model-implementation)](img/exploration-production.png){fig-align="center" width=70%}

- [**How?**]{.orange}
    - Applying [**software development best practices**]{.blue2}
    - Introducing [**new concepts and tools**]{.blue2}: [DataOps](https://dataopsmanifesto.org/en/) and MLOps

## DataOps and MLOps

- Origin: the [DevOps](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/DevOps) movement

. . .

![Source: [syloe.com](https://www.syloe.com/expert-devops-automatisation-deploiements/)](img/devops.png){fig-align="center" height=300}

- [**DataOps**]{.orange}: building robust [**data pipelines**]{.blue2}

- [**MLOps**]{.orange}: deploying and maintaining [**ML models**]{.blue2}


# Handling data driven projects distinct lifecycles {.inverse}

## Three lifecycles to master

A robust ML pipeline requires managing [**three independent lifecycles**]{.orange}:

. . .

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="33%"}
**Data**

- Versioning
- Lineage & provenance
- Quality checks
:::
::: {.column width="33%"}
**Code**

- Version control (Git)
- Modular design
- Automated testing
:::
::: {.column width="33%"}
**Software environment**

- Dependency pinning
- Reproducibility
- Portability
:::
:::

## Why does this matter?

- Models are [**not static artefacts**]{.blue2}: they depend on data, code, *and* the environment in which they were trained

. . .

- A model result is [**only reproducible**]{.orange} if all three are tracked together

. . .

- Failing to manage even one lifecycle leads to:
    - *"Works on my machine"* syndrome
    - Inability to roll back a broken update
    - Silent regressions caused by data or dependency drift

## Tooling for lifecycle management

| Lifecycle | Tools |
|-----------|-------|
| Data | DVC, Delta Lake, Great Expectations |
| Code | Git, pre-commit hooks, CI pipelines |
| Environment | pip-tools, conda-lock, Poetry |

→ Combining all three gives you a [**fully reproducible and auditable pipeline**]{.orange}

---

# Part 2 — Containerisation and Kubernetes {.inverse}

## Bridging the dev/production gap

The [**environment gap**]{.orange} is one of the most common sources of failure:

> *"It worked on my laptop"* — but production uses different OS, libraries, CUDA versions…

. . .

[**Containerisation**]{.blue2} (Docker) solves this by packaging the full runtime alongside the code:

- Same image runs locally, in CI, and in production
- Eliminates *"it depends on the machine"* class of bugs

## From a container to a fleet

| Concern | Docker alone | Docker + Kubernetes |
|---------|-------------|---------------------|
| Single service | ✅ | ✅ |
| Scaling on demand | ❌ | ✅ |
| Self-healing | ❌ | ✅ |
| Rolling updates | ❌ | ✅ |
| Resource scheduling | ❌ | ✅ |

[**Kubernetes**]{.orange} turns individual containers into an [**industrialised, scalable fleet**]{.blue2}

## MLOps on Kubernetes

- [**Training**]{.orange}: launch distributed GPU jobs (Kubeflow, Argo Workflows)
- [**Model serving**]{.orange}: expose a versioned model behind a stable endpoint (KServe, Seldon)
- [**Canary deployments**]{.orange}: route a fraction of traffic to a new model version before full rollout
- [**Rollbacks**]{.orange}: switch back to a previous version in seconds if performance degrades

---

# Part 3 — ML Platforms: experiment tracking and observability {.inverse}

## The experimentation phase

During development, practitioners need to:

- Track [**every experiment**]{.orange}: hyperparameters, metrics, artefacts
- [**Compare runs**]{.blue2} objectively
- [**Select and register**]{.blue2} the best model version

. . .

![](img/mlflow-ui.png){fig-align="center" height=280}

[**MLflow**]{.orange} provides a centralised tracking server, model registry, and serving API

## From experimentation to production observability

The same platforms extend into [**production monitoring**]{.orange}:

- Log [**real-world inputs and outputs**]{.blue2}
- Compute [**performance metrics**]{.blue2} against ground truth or human feedback
- Detect [**data drift**]{.orange} and [**concept drift**]{.orange}

. . .

For LLM-based systems, [**Langfuse**]{.blue2} adds:

- Trace-level observability (prompt → retrieval → generation)
- Cost and latency tracking
- Human annotation workflows

## Feedback loops and continuous improvement

```
Deploy → Monitor → Detect drift → Collect new labels → Retrain → Deploy
   ↑___________________________________________________|
```

. . .

- [**Monitoring is not optional**]{.orange}: a model that worked at launch will degrade as the world changes
- [**Feedback loops**]{.blue2} close the gap between offline evaluation and real-world performance
- Good observability turns every production incident into a [**training signal**]{.blue2}

---

# Part 4 — Supervised learning vs. LLM-based systems {.inverse}

## Two paradigms, two sets of operational constraints

|  | Supervised ML | LLM-based systems |
|--|--------------|-------------------|
| Training | Full retraining cycle | Fine-tuning or prompt engineering |
| Evaluation | Standard metrics (F1, RMSE…) | Requires LLM-as-judge or human review |
| Drift | Feature / label drift | Prompt drift, context drift |
| Versioning | Model weights + data | Weights + prompts + RAG index |
| Latency | Typically low | Higher, variable |

## Annotation and evaluation challenges

[**Supervised learning**]{.orange}:

- Ground truth is (relatively) well-defined
- Active learning can reduce annotation cost
- Evaluation is largely automated

. . .

[**LLMs**]{.orange}:

- What is the "correct" output? Often ambiguous
- Human evaluation is expensive and hard to scale
- Automated evaluation (LLM-as-judge) introduces its own biases
- Prompt changes can silently break previously passing evaluations

## Robustness and lifecycle management

[**Supervised ML**]{.blue2}:

- Risk: data drift, covariate shift
- Mitigation: monitoring distributions, scheduled retraining

. . .

[**LLM-based systems**]{.blue2}:

- Risk: hallucination, prompt injection, context window limits
- Mitigation: guardrails, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), eval regression suites
- Lifecycle: model upgrades from providers require [**re-evaluation of the full system**]{.orange}, not just the model

---

# Conclusion {.inverse}

## Key takeaways

1. [**Structure your project**]{.orange} around three independent lifecycles: data, code, environment
2. [**Containerise early**]{.orange}: it is the single most effective step toward reproducibility and portability
3. [**Track everything**]{.orange}: experiments, models, prompts — if it's not tracked, it didn't happen
4. [**Monitor in production**]{.orange}: evaluation does not stop at deployment
5. [**Know your paradigm**]{.orange}: supervised ML and LLM-based systems require different tooling and processes

. . .

> The gap between a notebook that works and a system that delivers value is not a technical gap — it is an [**operational**]{.blue2} one.

## References and further reading

- [MLflow documentation](https://mlflow.org/docs/latest/index.html)
- [Langfuse documentation](https://langfuse.com/docs)
- [Kubeflow](https://www.kubeflow.org/) — ML workflows on Kubernetes
- [DataOps Manifesto](https://dataopsmanifesto.org/en/)
- Sculley et al., *Hidden Technical Debt in Machine Learning Systems* (NeurIPS 2015)
- Huyen, *Designing Machine Learning Systems* (O'Reilly, 2022)
